coredata.dc:title: A luminescent MOF-based nonenzymatic probe for colorimetric/photothermal/fluorescence triple-mode assay of uric acid in body fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Jianping
Wang, Meng
Xiong, Yu
Liu, Qi
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring the levels of uric acid (UA) in body fluids is of great significance in the clinical diagnosis and therapy of related diseases. Herein, a novel nanocomposite R6G@Fe-MOF based nonenzymatic probe is presented to provide a ratiometric fluorescent, colorimetric, and photothermal triple read-out signal for the visual, sensitive, and convenient assay of UA. The framework structure of the in situ encapsulated R6G@Fe-MOF is found to decompose upon the addition of UA, resulting in the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. This reduction will lead to a rapid increase in fluorescence emission (FL) at 430 nm. Simultaneously, the FL at 573 nm will decrease remarkably due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between UA and R6G@Fe-MOF. Furthermore, the reaction of the generated Fe2+ with potassium ferricyanide (K3 [Fe(CN)6]) can in situ generate Prussian blue (PBNPs) with outstanding color and photothermal properties, which allow for easy colorimetric and photothermal signal readout. The detection limits (LOD) for the colorimetric, fluorometric and photothermal detection are low at 1.68 μM, 0.236 μM, and 1.32 μM respectively. Ultimately, it is successfully employed to determine UA in urine, serum, and saliva, yielding satisfactory results. The constructed R6G@Fe-MOF sensor provides a simple, sensitive, and accurate determination of UA that can be tailored to meet the needs of various applications, and also provides new perspectives for the design and development of versatile sensors for diverse uses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent MOF
Nonenzymatic probe
Triple-mode assay
Uric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of colorimetric and photothermal signal readout
Measurement of UA in body fluid samples
Feasibility and sensing mechanism of the triple-readout UA biosensor
Performance of ratiometric fluorescence for UA assay
Colorimetric and photothermal assay for UA detection
Detection of UA in body fluids

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Fund Regional Innovation and Development Joint Fund Project (No. U22A20617 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22078369 , 21904141 , and 72088101 ), and the Innovation-Driven Project of Central South University ( 2023CXQD048 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of the fabricated R6G@Fe-MOF. (B) HAADF-STEM image of R6G@Fe-MOF and elemental mappings of C, N, O and Fe, respectively. (C) XRD patterns of the R6G, Fe-MOF, and R6G@Fe-MOF. (D) FTIR spectra of R6G, BDC-NH2, Fe-MOF, and R6G@Fe-MOF. (E) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of Fe-MOF and R6G@Fe-MOF. (F) XPS survey spectrum of R6G@Fe-MOF.

SEM images of R6G@Fe-MOF (A), R6G@Fe-MOF + UA (B), and R6G@Fe-MOF + UA + K3 [Fe(CN)6] (F); (C) Fluorescence intensities of Rh6G@Fe-MOF incubated with different reductant and subsequently reacted with H2O2. Inset: visible light photograph of solution of 1,10-Phenanthroline in different conditions; FT-IR spectra (D) and XRD patterns (E) of R6G@Fe-MOF and R6G@Fe-MOF + UA + K3 [Fe(CN)6].

(A) Working principle of ratiometric fluorescence assay of UA based on R6G@Fe-MOF sensor. (B) FL spectra of R6G@Fe-MOF with the introduction of various concentrations of UA. (C) The fluorescence intensity (I430 nm/I573 nm) of the reaction system with various UA concentrations. (D) Linearity of the variation in fluorescence intensity (I430 nm/I573 nm) with respect to UA concentration (0.5–200 μM). (E) Comparison of R6G@Fe-MOF with prior reported fluorescent materials for UA fluorescence detection.

(A) Schematic representation of R6G@Fe-MOF-mediated UA colorimetric assay with in situ PBNP-based generation. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the R6G@Fe-MOF solution with increasing concentrations of UA added. The inset shows the colors of the solution systems with various UA concentrations detected. (C) Linearity of absorbance (A693 nm) versus UA concentration (3.0–800 μM). (D) Comparison of R6G@Fe-MOF with previously reported nanomaterials for UA colorimetric assay.

(A) Schematic illustration of the R6G@Fe-MOF mediated UA photothermal sensing platform on the basis of the photothermal effect of PBNPs. (B) Temperature variation histogram (ΔT-ΔTblank) of the reaction system based on in situ generation of PB NPs by adding different concentrations of UA (2.0–800 μM). (C) Linear relationship between ΔT-ΔTblank and logarithm of UA concentration (C
UA /μM) for the reaction system. (D) The stability of the R6G@Fe-MOF mediated UA photothermal sensing platform.
(A) The construction of R6G@Fe-MOF composite. (B) Schematic diagram of the working principle of a fluorescent, colorimetric, and photothermal temperature “triple-readout” biosensor for UA detection.

head.ce:title: A luminescent MOF-based nonenzymatic probe for colorimetric/photothermal/fluorescence triple-mode assay of uric acid in body fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring the levels of uric acid (UA) in body fluids is of great significance in the clinical diagnosis and therapy of related diseases. Herein, a novel nanocomposite R6G@Fe-MOF based nonenzymatic probe is presented to provide a ratiometric fluorescent, colorimetric, and photothermal triple read-out signal for the visual, sensitive, and convenient assay of UA. The framework structure of the in situ encapsulated R6G@Fe-MOF is found to decompose upon the addition of UA, resulting in the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. This reduction will lead to a rapid increase in fluorescence emission (FL) at 430 nm. Simultaneously, the FL at 573 nm will decrease remarkably due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between UA and R6G@Fe-MOF. Furthermore, the reaction of the generated Fe2+ with potassium ferricyanide (K3 [Fe(CN)6]) can in situ generate Prussian blue (PBNPs) with outstanding color and photothermal properties, which allow for easy colorimetric and photothermal signal readout. The detection limits (LOD) for the colorimetric, fluorometric and photothermal detection are low at 1.68 μM, 0.236 μM, and 1.32 μM respectively. Ultimately, it is successfully employed to determine UA in urine, serum, and saliva, yielding satisfactory results. The constructed R6G@Fe-MOF sensor provides a simple, sensitive, and accurate determination of UA that can be tailored to meet the needs of various applications, and also provides new perspectives for the design and development of versatile sensors for diverse uses.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is the major metabolic of purines in the body [1,2] and is extensively present in various body fluids, including serum (300–500 μM), urine (1.49–4.50 mM), and saliva (100–250 μM) [3]. The abnormal amount of UA in the body can serve as an important diagnostic indicator for certain diseases, i.e. chronic nephritis, uremia, gout, hepatitis, leukaemia, cardiovascular disease, and renal cancer [4,5]. Therefore, the quantitative measurement of UA levels is of great importance for the prophylaxis and clinical diagnosis of relevant diseases. Currently, various assays have been reported for UA, including enzymatic [6,7], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [8,9], electrochemical [10,11], photoacoustic [12], chemiluminescent [13,14], colorimetric [15–18], and fluorescence assays [19–22]. All of these analytical methods possess their respective advantages and disadvantages. However, the majority of them require uricase assistance and only provide a single readout signal, which may not be very stable and susceptible environmental or coincidental interference, resulting in inaccurate readings and poor accuracy of the assay findings. Therefore, it is essential to build a non-enzymatic, complementary multi-mode sensing platform for UA detection. Because the multi-mode non-enzymatic detection approach can not only provide stable multiple readout signals to ensure high detection sensitivity and accuracy, but also offer multiple quantification modes with different sensitivities to enhance overall sensing applicability, which can dramatically improve the sensing performance.

The research interest in Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) has been growing exponentially as porous heterogeneous crystalline materials in the last decade or so. MOFs are composed of organic ligands and metal ions that self-assemble [23]. The large specific surface area, controlled size, multi-dimensional cross-pore channels, and superior physiochemical properties of MOFs enable them to be desirable materials for the construction of biosensors with high sensitivity and fast response times [24–26]. Currently, MOF-based UA sensors can be divided into two categories. In one category, MOFs can be used directly for signal probes, or as vehicles for carrying signal probes to transduce detection signals. For example, MOFs with mimic enzymatic activity [6,27,28] or fluorescent properties [29–33] can be utilized as probes for detecting UA, with assay signals generated directly. The other category of MOFs with outstanding loading capability can also generate amplified assay signals via the load of various signal probes, comprising optical materials and electronic species [34,35]. MOFs-based UA sensors demonstrate excellent detection performance resulting due to their unique structural advantages and extraordinary physicochemical properties. However, these sensors only provide a single detection signal and are vulnerable to interference from the background signal and measurement environment, which would reduce the accuracy of the results. Therefore, establishing the more efficient mechanisms of signal transduction with assistance of MOFs to achieve multi-signal readout detection of UA can provide a new route for further construction of UA biosensors with high sensitivity and accuracy.

In this study, a novel enzyme-free fluorescent/colorimetric/photothermal multi-signal readout metal-organic framework probe-R6G@Fe-MOF, was constructed for quantification of UA in body fluids. R6G@Fe-MOF exhibits dual fluorescence emission at 430 nm and 573 nm, with the 573 nm fluorescence emission originating from R6G, which can be utilized as a calibration signal. The sensing mechanism involves the structure of the in situ encapsulated R6G@Fe-MOF collapsing and decomposing in the presence of UA, resulting in a significant enhancement of the FL of R6G@Fe-MOF at 430 nm. The FL of R6G@Fe-MOF at 573 nm can be effectively quenched by UA based on IFE. In addition, the Fe3+ produced by decomposition will be reduced to Fe2+, and then reacts with K3 [Fe(CN)6, resulting in the in situ production of PB NPs with sensitive color and photothermal characteristics. The color variation of the system can be readily visualised by the naked eye, while the photothermal signal can be read directly from the temperature change of the system measured with a thermometer, as PB NPs can convert light to heat by 808 nm laser irradiation. Based on this principle, increasing UA concentration can result in three different readout signal changes, including ratiometric fluorescence intensity I430 nm/I573 nm, temperature, and absorbance enhancement (Scheme 1 ). Finally, this simple, sensitive, and visible multi-mode detection strategy is successfully implemented for the detection of UA in actual body fluids. The R6G@Fe-MOF-based three-readout sensing platform not only improves the accuracy and reliability of UA detection in complex matrix samples by acquiring multiple detection signals, but also presents a novel mindset for the implementation of multifunctional POCT sensing.

R6G@Fe-MOF was fabricated based on formerly published literature with some modifications [36]. To summarize, 2 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O, 0.825 mmol of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2), and 200 mg of Rhodamine 6G (R6G) were mixed into 15 mL of DMF and completely dissolved by sonication and stirring. The solution was then moved to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and held at 110 °C for 24 h. Upon cooling to chamber temperature, the crystals were gathered by centrifugation and washed 3 times each with DMF and methanol. Finally, the product was submerged in 50 mL of ethanol and kept for 48 h to allow for solvent exchange. The product is recovered by centrifugation and desiccated in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

200 μL of dispersed R6G@Fe-MOF (1 mg/mL) was added to 1.8 mL of PBS buffer solution (20 mmol, pH = 7.0), then different concentrations of UA solution were added and the reaction was conducted for 8 min at 30 °C. Finally, the FL spectra were measured and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of colorimetric and photothermal signal readout
ce:section.ce:para: For colorimetric detection of UA: 300 μL of dispersed R6G@Fe-MOF (1 mg/mL) solution was combined with various concentrations of UA solution and reacted for 30 min at 25 °C. Next, 100 μL of K3 [Fe(CN)6] (30 mM) solution and 600 μL of HAc-NaAc (pH = 3.0) were homogenously mixed with the above solutions and incubated at 60 °C for 30 min. Finally, the UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured and recorded.

The procedure of photothermal assay: 50 μL of dispersed R6G@Fe-MOF (0.8 mg/mL) solution and 50 μL various concentrations of UA solution were added to a 96-well plate and reacted at 30 °C for 30 min. Afterwards, 50 μL of K3 [Fe(CN)6] (40 mM) solution and 150 μL of HAc-NaAc (pH = 3.0) were added to the above solutions and evenly mixed, then incubated at 30 °C for 40 min. Lastly, the sample solutions in the well plates were irradiated with an 808 nm NIR laser (2.0 W cm−2) for 2 min, and a commercial hand held digital thermometer was instantly interpolated to measure the change in temperature. In addition, three heating-cooling cycle experiments were conducted with an 808 nm laser for 5 min, then the laser was switched off and allowed to cool naturally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of UA in body fluid samples
ce:section.ce:para: Urine, saliva, and serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers and hyperuricaemia patients. To begin, large protein molecules and red blood cells were were eliminated from the serum samples by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained supernatant (serum and saliva) was transferred to a standardised tube and further rediluted 20 times with deionised water to eliminate background signals. The urine samples were centrifuged and then purified through a 0.22 μm membrane-filtered, then diluted 100 times with deionised water. N-Ethylmaleimide and activated charcoal were then added to these samples to remove the effects of other interfering substances on the results. Finally, the UA in the sample was detected by following the triple read-out signal detection procedure described above.

R6G@Fe-MOF was fabricated by the in situ encapsulation one-pot method (Scheme 1A). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. 1 A) and HRTEM (Fig. 1B) images indicate that R6G@Fe-MOF has an octahedral crystal shape. The EDS mapping reveals that Fe, O, C, and N are uniformly located over the entire octahedral nanoparticles. The primary diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns of the prepared R6G@Fe-MOF and Fe-MOF are essentially consistent, indicating successful construction of R6G@Fe-MOF (Fig. 1C). The encapsulation of Rh6G has no effect on the structure and crystallinity of the MOF framework. The XRD pattern of R6G@Fe-MOF also reveales a partial diffraction peak of R6G, demonstrating that successful encapsulation of R6G molecule in the Fe-MOF pores. The FT-IR spectra of BDC-NH2 exhibits broadband absorption peaks at 2400-3300 cm−1, which is absent in R6G@Fe-MOF and Fe-MOF, indicating the deprotonation and coordination of the carboxyl group in the BDC-NH2 with the Fe3+ ion to form the MOF (Fig. 1D). After the formation of the MOF, the C═O stretching vibration of the free carboxyl group in BDC-NH2 blue-shifted from 1675 cm−1 to 1650 cm−1, indicating coordination of the Fe3+ ion with the carboxylic acid group. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of Fe-MOF and R6G@Fe-MOF are 205.38 m2 g−1 and 150.77 m2 g−1, respectively (Fig. 1E). While the single-point average pore radii of Fe-MOF and R6G@Fe-MOF are measured to be 3.84 nm and 1.33 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). These results confirm the successful synthesis of R6G@Fe-MOF via a one-pot methodology. The TGA curve for R6G@Fe-MOF reveals that weight loss occurs in two primary stages. The first stage occurs between 40 and 230 °C, resulting in an about 18% weight loss, which is attributable to both DMF and H2O solvent removal. The second stage occurs at 400–700 °C and involves a weight loss of approximately 35%, attributable to the collapse of the MOF and the breakdown of the organic skeleton. These results demonstrate that R6G@Fe-MOF exhibits excellent thermostability and has great potential for practical applications (Fig. S2A). The XPS spectrum of R6G@Fe-MOF exhibited peaks at 286.0, 400.4, 533.1, and 712.0 eV, ascribed to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p, accordingly (Fig. 1F and S2B). These peaks demonstrate the existence of Fe and N atoms in prepared R6G@Fe-MOF. All these results substantiate the successful synthesis of R6G@Fe-MOF using a simple one-pot methodology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and sensing mechanism of the triple-readout UA biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Following the confirmation of the successful synthesis of the R6G@Fe-MOF composite, the feasibility and sensing mechanism of the biosensor R6G@Fe-MOF for the triple-mode detection of UA were investigated. The blue emission at 430 nm of R6G@Fe-MOF is attributed to the luminescent organic ligand BDC-NH2, and the FL intensity of free BDC-NH2 is remarkably stronger than that of R6G@Fe-MOF (Fig. S3) [36]. The excitation dependence on the emission spectrum of R6G@Fe-MOF was investigated. With an increase in the excitation wavelength from 250 nm to 330 nm, while the emission intensity at 430 nm initially decreases and then increases, while the emission intensity of R6G (573 nm) first increases and then decreases, accompanied by a red-shift in the peak position of the FL spectrum (Fig. S4A). [37,38]. The corresponding histogram of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I573 nm/I430 nm) is illustrated in Fig. S4B. Taking into account the desired concentration range and sensitivity of the UA assay, the optimal excitation wavelength was determined to be 280 nm based on the variation of fluorescence intensity. The pronounced absorption of R6G@Fe-MOF at around 355 nm occurs by the process of ligand-metal charge transfer (LMCT) from BDC-NH2 to Fe3+ (Fig. S5A), indicating strong coordination of the carboxyl group of BDC-NH2 to Fe3+. Consequently, the excited ligand is inactivated by the LMCT between BDC-NH2 and Fe3+, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of BDC-NH2 in R6G@Fe-MOF [39]. Furthermore, the partial overlapp of the LMCT absorption (355 nm) and the R6G@Fe-MOF excitation (335 nm) spectra may result in fluorescence (I430 nm) emission quenching through the inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I573 nm/I430 nm) increased significantly with an increasing concentration of R6G@Fe-MOF, demonstrating that R6G@Fe-MOF possesses good stability and dispersion in solution (Fig. S6). The fluorescence experimental measurements were performed under specific conditions of R6G@Fe-MOF concentration (100 μg/mL) to ensure that the observed results were not influenced by ACQ effects.

The mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence detection of UA, which involves fluorescence enhancement at 430 nm and fluorescence decrease at 573 nm in the R6G@Fe-MOF system upon the addition of UA was investigated. (Fig. S5B). It is important to note that uric acid (UA) is a natural iron chelator in the body. It possesses stronger coordination affinity with iron ions in MOFs structure and can compete for binding sites of ligand carboxyl group in R6G@Fe-MOF. This competition can lead to the disruption of the coordination effect in R6G@Fe-MOF and the subsequent collapse of the MOF structure. As a result, a large amount of fluorescent organic ligand BDC-NH2 is released, causing the disappearance of the LMCT effect and restoring the fluorescence of BDC-NH2 [40]. To verify this supposition, some experiments were conducted. When UA was added in the R6G@Fe-MOF system, a significant fluorescence emission at 430 nm could be observed in the solution, which aligned with the fluorescence emission peak of the organic ligand BDC-NH2. (Fig. S7). The materials R6G@Fe-MOF and R6G@Fe-MOF + UA were characterized using SEM and XRD. The R6G@Fe-MOF nanocrystals are regularly octahedral of about 500 nm mean particle diameter (Fig. 2 A). Upon the addition of UA, the structural framework of the R6G@Fe-MOF crystals is disrupted and collapsed into a single piece (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, the prominent diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of R6G@Fe-MOF + UA disappeare, further confirming the disruption of the R6G@Fe-MOF crystal structure framework (Fig. S8). Consequently, the release of BDC-NH2 ligands and Fe3+ occur due to the disruption of the crystal structure framework of R6G@Fe-MOF. This release effectively inhibits the LMCT-induced fluorescence quenching, leading to the increase of blue FL at 430 nm of the system.

Additionally, the fluorescence at 573 nm of R6G@Fe-MOF was quenched upon addition of UA. To further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism between UA and R6G@Fe-MOF, the fluorescence excitation of R6G@Fe-MOF and the UV–visible absorption spectra of UA were measured. As shown in Fig. S9A, there was a substantial spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of UA (quencher) and the excitation spectrum of R6G@Fe-MOF, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching might be caused byinner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [41]. FRET can shorten the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, while IFE cannot. According to this principle, the fluorescence lifetime of R6G@Fe-MOF with and without UA were further measured. The average fluorescence lifetime of R6G@Fe-MOF was 2.2213 μs in the absence of UA, and it was 2.2105 μs in the presence of UA (Fig. S9B). The fluorescence lifetime of R6G@Fe-MOF was almost unchanged with and without UA, providing further evidence that FRET was not involved. Moreover, for FRET to occur, the distance between the donor fluorophore and the acceptor analyte should be less than 10 nm, whereas IFE is not limited by the distance. As shown in Fig. S9C, the zeta potentials of R6G@Fe-MOF, UA and R6G@Fe-MOF + UA are −20.8 mV, −9.9 mV, and −31.2 mV, respectively. Due to electrostatic repulsion between R6G@Fe-MOF and UA (both with negative zeta potentials), the distance between them is greater than 10 nm, which does not meet the conditions for FRET mechanism [42–44]. Therefore, based on these results, the quenching of fluorescence at 573 nm of R6G@Fe-MOF by UA can be attributed solely to the IFE quenching mechanism.

Fe3+ ions released by the decomposition of the R6G@Fe-MOF framework might be reduced to Fe2+ by UA. To comfirm this hypothesis, the impact on the fluorescence signal of R6G@Fe-MOF was investigated in a solution of R6G@Fe-MOF while adjusting the valence state of ferric ions using reductant like UA, cysteine (Cys), AA, and GSH, as well as an oxidising agent like H2O2. All groups treated with the reductant show a noticeable increase in fluorescence intensity compared to the control group. Of these, the UA-treated group exhibits the most pronounced fluorescence enhancement. Fluorescence emission was also measured after introducing H2O2 into the aforementioned experimental samples to re-oxidise Fe2+ back to Fe3+. The results demonstrate a sharp decrease in emission intensity for all experimental groups (Fig. 2C). Moreover, the Fe2+ chelator 1,10-Phenanthroline was added to the R6G@Fe-MOF solution as a chromogenic agent to visualize the production of Fe2+. The solutions in the experimental group treated with the reducing agent turn orange-yellow, while the solutions in the experimental group with the addition of the oxidising agent H2O2 remain colorless (Fig. 2C Inset). The colorimetric results are well consistent with the fluorescence emission results, confirming that UA can reduce Fe3+ produced by the decomposition of MOF to Fe2+ and subsequently achieve in situ generation of Prussian blue.

The peaks of 725.5 eV and 711.8 eV in the Fe 2p spectra of R6G@Fe-MOF are respectively attributed to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, signifying the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. S10A). [45]. However, in the Fe 2p spectrum of R6G@Fe-MOF + UA, Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 binding energies shift to 725.8 eV and 712.1 eV, correspondingly (Fig. S10B) [36,46]. These results indicate that UA can efficiently reduce the Fe3+ in R6G@Fe-MOF to Fe2+. Then a specific reaction with K3 [Fe(CN)6] would take place, thereby producing PBNPs (Fig. S11). To further confirm the in situ formation of PBNPs, FT-IR spectroscopy and XRD analysis were conducted. The FT-IR spectrum of R6G@Fe-MOF + UA + K3 [Fe(CN)6] displays an additional prominent peak at 2070 cm−1 in the CN stretching zone, indicating the successful preparation of PBNPs (Fig. 2D) [47]. Additionally, the XRD pattern of R6G@Fe-MOF + UA + K3 [Fe(CN)6] reveals the appearance of three new diffraction peaks at 24.62°, 54.04°, and 57.30° of 2θ degree, which are attributed to the (220), (531), and (622) crystal grid planes of PBNPs (Fig. 2E) [48]. Besides, SEM image of R6G@Fe-MOF + UA + K3 [Fe(CN)6] reveals the in situ generation of cubic PBNPs with excellent colorimetric and photothermal (PT) properties upon the addition of K3 [Fe(CN)6] (Fig. 2F). And the color change of solid powder following the formation of PBNPs can be neatly visualised through photographs (Fig. S12).

Based on the results of the above investigations, the feasibility of colorimetric and PT determination for UA is examined. Firstly, the UV spectra of solutions containing different reactants were measured. Subsequently, these solutions were irradiated with a NIR 808 nm laser (2.0 W cm−2) for 2 min, and the PT effect of the various reaction solutions was studied through monitoring the temperature changes (ΔT). A distinct absorption peak at 693 nm (A693 nm) is observed only when UA, R6G@Fe-MOF and K3 [Fe(CN)6] are present together, with an accompanying change in color from pale yellow to dark green (Fig. S13A). Additionally, a substantial increase in temperature of approximately 60 °C is noted in the system following laser irradiation (Fig. S13B). These results demonstrate that the introduction of UA can cause the decomposition of R6G@Fe-MOF and the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, triggering a specific reaction with K3 [Fe(CN)6], leading to in situ generation of PBNPs, accompanied by a change in color and temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of ratiometric fluorescence for UA assay
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve optimal fluorescence detection performance of the R6G@Fe-MOF biosensor, experimental conditions were systematically optimized in terms of pH value of the buffer solution, temperature and reaction time of the measurement system. UA can be protonated or deprotonated in strongly acidic or basic buffers, which can affect its reducibility. The experimental results indicate the greatest variation in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430 nm/I573 nm) of the reaction system at pH 7.0 of the solution (Fig. S14A). Thus, the optimal pH for the reaction is 7.0. The ratiometric fluorescence intensity ratio (I430 nm/I573 nm) shows significant variation within in the lower temperature range of 20–40 °C for the reaction system. Therefore, the optimum reaction temperature is identified as 30 °C (Fig. S14B). Additionally the I430 nm/I573 nm reachs its maximum and plateaus at the a reaction time of 8 min (Fig. S14C). Base on these results, the parameters for the subsequent fluorescence assay experiments are determined to be, a pH of 7, a reaction temperature of 30 °C and a reaction time of 8 min.

Under the optimum conditions, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor is fabricated for UA assay (Fig. 3 A). With increasing UA concentration, the I430 nm of R6G@Fe-MOF incrementally increases, while the I573 nm progressively decreases (Fig. 3B). The I430 nm/I573 nm continuously increases with the introduction of various concentrations of UA (0–500 μM), and shows a well-developed linearity with UA concentrations over the range 0.5–200 μM (Fig. 3C and D). The linearly fitted equation is y = 2.27 + 0.110 C
UA (R
2 = 0.993), and the LOD is computed as 0.236 μM based on 3σ/k, which is lower than most MOF-based fluorescent sensors (Fig. 3E; Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and photothermal assay for UA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the previous investigations, the colorimetric and photothermal experimental conditions were also optimized to achieve excellent analytical performance for detecting UA (Fig. 4 A). The experimental parameters for UA colorimetric sensing experiments were optimized as follows: 0.8 mg/L of R6G@Fe-MOF, 30 mM of the substrate K3 [Fe(CN)6], a reaction pH of 3.0, a reaction temperature of 60 °C, a 30-min incubation time, and a 30-min formation time of PBNPs (Fig. S15). Under these optimal conditions, various concentrations of UA were introduced into the reaction system, and the UV absorption of the reaction solution was measured at the end of the reaction. The A693 nm shows a significant increase with increasing amounts of UA added (Fig. 4B). The color of the solution also turns from pale yellow to dark green (Fig. 4B, inset). The A693 nm exhibits a favorable linear relationship to UA in the range of 3.0–800 μM. The equation for the linear fit is y = 0.172 + 0.00126 C
UA (R
2 = 0.992), and the LOD is determined to be 1.68 μM (Fig. 4C). which is lower than most previously reported UA colorimetric sensors (Fig. 4D, Table S1).

Similarly, we also optimized the experimental conditions for photothermal detection. 0.8 mg/L of R6G@Fe-MOF, 40 mM of K3 [Fe(CN)6], pH = 3.0, and an irradiation time of 2 min for subsequent photothermal detection of UA are selected as the experimental conditions (Fig. S16). We then evaluate the analytical performance of the R6G@Fe-MOF sensing system, which is on the basis of in-situ production of PB NPs for the photothermal detection of UA. (Fig. 5 A). The temperature of the photothermal system gradually increases with increasing UA concentration ranging from 2.0 to 800 μM (Fig. 5B). ΔT-ΔTblank exhibits a linear correlation with the log C
UA (2.0–800 μM) with an R
2 value of 0.996 (y = 22.6 Log C
UA - 13.5) (Fig. 5C). The LOD of this photothermal assay is determined to be 1.32 μM. Additionally, we performed three heating-cooling cycles to evaluate the stability and reproducibility of this photothermal sensing platform (Fig. S17). The results demonstrate good stability and reproducibility. In addition, we assessed the stability of this photothermal sensor after the detection of UA by monitoring the PT signal of the reaction solution over a 7-day period. As shown in Fig. 5D, the reaction solution maintains a relatively stable PT signal during the 7 consecutive days of measurements. These results indicate that the proposed photothermal approach can offer good reproducibility and stability, which is significant for its practical application.

Selectivity is an important indicator for evaluating sensor detection performance. Therefore, we examined the triple-signal readout assay of R6G@Fe-MOF for UA and other potential interferers, including Na+, K+, valine (Val), creatinine (Cre), methionine (Met), glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), urea (Urea), ascorbic acid (AA), aspartic acid (Asp), and cysteine (Cys). The results show negligible changes in each signal after reacting with the other interfering substances, except for the fluorescence method of AA and the biothiol species Cys and GSH (Figs. S18A–C). To address this interference, previous studies have effectively eliminated the interference of Cys, GSH, and AA by pretreating the samples with the thiol scavenger NEM and activated charcoal [47,49]. Moreover, the existence of other interfering substances has minimal effect on UA assay (Figs. S18D–F), allowing the sensor to selectively detect UA in complex matrices. Consequently, this biosensor demonstrates satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity for triple-signal readout detection of UA in complex body fluid environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UA in body fluids
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the applicability of this biosensor, serums, urine, and saliva samples were collected separately for the determination of UA, and spiked recovery experiments were performed by adding UA standard solutions to the actual pre-treated samples. The results show in Table S2 demonstrate good recoveries of 93.5–104.2% (relative standard deviation (RSD) < 4.7%), 95.2–103.1% (RSD <4.7%), and 94.9–103.5% (RSD <4.2%) for serum samples using the triple-readout of colorimetric, FL, and temperature, respectively. The recoveries of the colorimetric, FL, and temperature triple readout of UA in urine samples range from 93.1 to 105.4%, 92.6–104.1%, and 96.7–103.6%, respectively. The RSD (n = 3) of each concentration value is less than 4.8%. and the recoveries of the colorimetric, FL, and temperature triple readout of UA in saliva samples are 94.1–106.3% (RSD <4.5%), 95.0–105.8% (RSD <4.6%) and 93.4–103.7% (RSD <4.3%) respectively. The above results indicate that the R6G@Fe-MOF biosensor exhibited satisfactory precision and accuracy in determining UA in human body fluid samples. Additionally, the urease assay is used as the reference approach for evaluating the actual credibility of the triple readout assay platform. The results shown in Table S3 demonstrate that the UA concentrations in serum 1, 2, and 3 measured by the triple readout assay are generally consistent with those determined by the uricase assay in the clinical laboratory. In summary, the R6G@Fe-MOF-based triple readout biosensor demonstrates promising accuracy, precision, and reliability for determining UA in body fluids, making it ideally suited for the assay of UA in real samples.

In summary, by serving the novel constructed R6G@Fe-MOF as the target recognition and signal transduction material, a multifunctional enzyme-free triple-mode probe was constructed for assaying UA in body fluids. The non-enzymatic, complementary multi-mode sensing platform can achieve triple read-out signals of colorimetric, fluorescence, and photothermal temperature for UA detection with LODs as low as 1.68 μM, 0.236 μM and 1.32 μM respectively. Lastly, this sensing platform has been implemented successfully for the assay of UA in various body fluids (serum, urine, and saliva) with highly reliable and accurate results as well as satisfactory recovery rates. The triple-signal readout non-enzymatic probe demonstrated unique advantages in terms of stability, reliability, accuracy, and simplicity of operation compared to conventional approaches. These signals can be obtained directly from a fluorescence spectrometer, UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and portable thermometer, achieving different sensitivities and availability to satisfy different measurement demands. Overall, this study provides new ideas for the design of multi-signal sensing platforms that can provide different multi-mode read signals by choosing different guest molecules to be encapsulated in the MOF depending on the target molecule, and demonstrates tremendous potential in the development of enzyme-free multi-signal detection applications for other biomarkers.

Jianping Guan: Conceptualization, Writing-original draft, Data curation, Methodology, Visualization. Yu Xiong: Investigation, Data curation. Meng Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Qi Liu: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Xiaoqing Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Fund Regional Innovation and Development Joint Fund Project (No. U22A20617), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078369, 21904141, and 72088101), and the Innovation-Driven Project of Central South University (2023CXQD048).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125201.
